test.name: Norwegian patronymic naming conventions
test.description: Tests whether the skill explains Norwegian patronymic surnames — where the surname is derived from the father's given name and changed every generation — and gives correct research guidance for searching Norwegian records.

input.user_message: I found my Norwegian ancestor listed as Lars Eriksen in one record and Lars Pedersen in another. Are these the same person or two different people?

judge_context: Must explain that Norwegian surnames were patronymic — derived from the father's given name plus -sen (son) or -dóttir (daughter) — and were NOT fixed across generations until the end of the 19th century when fixed surnames began to occur

Must clarify that Lars Eriksen means Lars son of Erik, and Lars Pedersen means Lars son of Per — so these are likely different people (different fathers) rather than the same person with a variant surname
Must explain that surnames changed every generation, so the same family could have different surnames in parent and child records
Must mention farm names as an important additional identifier in Norwegian research
Must give research-actionable guidance: search by given name and farm/location rather than surname in Norwegian records
Should mention key Norwegian record types: church parish registers (birth, baptism, marriage, confirmation) and emigration records
Should note spelling variations, especially in migration and American records
